citations.narrative:
According to the facility’s records reviewed by the APHIS official during this inspection, a 10-month-old, male, hound-mix
dog (Animal ID #: E965), weighing approximately 23.8 kilograms, was placed under anesthesia on July 31, 2025 using a
combination of ketamine and propofol via the Total Intravenous Anesthesia (TIVA) method utilizing a programmable
syringe pump for the purpose of conducting a terminal procedure under protocol #: IS0007826. The veterinary technician
programmed the syringe pump and passed the IV line to the veterinarian, who connected it to the animal’s intravenous
catheter. A few minutes later, the veterinarian noticed that the animal’s vitals were abnormal as exemplified by low end-
tidal carbon dioxide, and the animal suddenly had very pale mucous membranes. The vet team started to troubleshoot the
problem, and during the process of assessing the animal, discovered that the heartbeat was absent as it could not be
found when auscultated or palpated. The veterinarian assessed the animal for corneal reflexes, which were absent
indicating the animal had passed away. The veterinary and laboratory teams continued to search for a cause of death.
During this process, the veterinary technician noticed the syringe on the pump was completely empty, indicating the
animal had received a large volume of anesthetic. Furthermore, it was determined that this happened due to the syringe
pump’s rate having been set to 8 ml/minute instead of 8 ml/hour as described in the study protocol. Thus, the facility
personnel determined that the cause of death was anesthesia overdose caused by incorrect setting of the syringe pump.
Failure to handle animals in a careful manner can negatively impact their health and well-being by resulting in injury,
death, etc. Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not
cause trauma, overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.
Correct by: February 9, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.38(f)(1)  Critical
   
Miscellaneous. 
According to the facility’s records reviewed by the APHIS official during this inspection, a 10-month-old, male, hound-mix 
dog (Animal ID #: E965), weighing approximately 23.8 kilograms, was placed under anesthesia on July 31, 2025 using a 
combination of ketamine and propofol via the Total Intravenous Anesthesia (TIVA) method utilizing a programmable 
syringe pump for the purpose of conducting a terminal procedure under protocol #: IS0007826. The veterinary technician 
programmed the syringe pump and passed the IV line to the veterinarian, who connected it to the animal’s intravenous 
catheter. A few minutes later, the veterinarian noticed that the animal’s vitals were abnormal as exemplified by low end-
tidal carbon dioxide, and the animal suddenly had very pale mucous membranes. The vet team started to troubleshoot the 
problem, and during the process of assessing the animal, discovered that the heartbeat was absent as it could not be 
found when auscultated or palpated. The veterinarian assessed the animal for corneal reflexes, which were absent 
indicating the animal had passed away. The veterinary and laboratory teams continued to search for a cause of death. 
During this process, the veterinary technician noticed the syringe on the pump was completely empty, indicating the 
animal had received a large volume of anesthetic. Furthermore, it was determined that this happened due to the syringe 
pump’s rate having been set to 8 ml/minute instead of 8 ml/hour as described in the study protocol. Thus, the facility 
personnel determined that the cause of death was anesthesia overdose caused by incorrect setting of the syringe pump. 
Failure to handle animals in a careful manner can negatively impact their health and well-being by resulting in injury, 
death, etc. Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not 
cause trauma, overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.  
 
Correct by: February 9, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
